steps.title: Identify the sentence structure around the blank
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses separated by the blank. Before: “The Limón technique… is known for its emphasis on breath control and its interplay of weight and [blank]” — a complete main clause. After: “dancers may explore, for example, the moment of mid-air suspension at the top of a jump” — another complete main clause. Two independent clauses must be joined by a boundary that is stronger than a comma and does not use a relative pronoun.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘weightlessness’: Placing no punctuation between two independent clauses creates a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘weightlessness which’: The relative pronoun “which” cannot join two independent clauses; it would need to modify a noun and creates a grammatically confused structure. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘weightlessness,’: A comma alone between two independent clauses without a coordinating conjunction is a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘weightlessness;’: A semicolon correctly separates two related independent clauses. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
